worth_it_explanation: A malicious creative that slips through reaches the publisher's own audience, so the cost of a gap here lands on user trust and on the publisher's standing with the demand partners who route through the platform. The check is a review of the creative scanning and blocking controls a publisher already has in Microsoft Monetize, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete control the publisher can turn on or tighten.
intro: Creative quality scanning decides whether a malicious or policy-violating ad reaches the publisher's audience or gets stopped before it serves. Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) carries demand from many sources, and any one of those creatives can carry malware, an auto-redirect that hijacks the page, or content that breaks the publisher's own policy. This subcategory is about whether scanning and blocking are switched on and configured to cover the inventory, and whether the platform is set to act on what it finds rather than log it and serve the ad anyway. These checks confirm that the protection a publisher relies on is actually doing the work the publisher assumes it is.

audit_checks.title: Creative scanning for malware and auto-redirects is not enabled across inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A creative carrying malware or an auto-redirect that reaches the publisher's audience harms the users the publisher is responsible for and damages the publisher's standing with the demand partners routing through the platform. When scanning is off, partial, or never confirmed, those creatives can serve unchecked, so the gap exposes real users rather than only costing revenue.

educate.base:
Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) carries demand from many sources, and any creative arriving from that demand can carry a problem the publisher never chose to serve. The three that matter most for a publisher are malware embedded in the creative, an auto-redirect that pulls the user off the page without a click, and content that violates the publisher's own policy. Creative scanning is the control that inspects a creative before or as it serves and stops the ones that fail, so the publisher's audience does not become the test surface for whatever the demand side sent.

Scanning happens in more than one place, and a publisher relying on one layer can have a gap in another. Microsoft Monetize applies its own creative auditing and quality controls to the demand flowing through the platform. Because Microsoft Monetize is Prebid-compatible, a publisher running header bidding can also add a pre-auction or real-time scanning module, such as the Confiant or GeoEdge integrations documented for Prebid, that inspects bids before they are allowed to win. The Prebid project is explicit that Prebid Server itself does not perform ad quality management, so the scanning has to come from the platform's own controls or an added module rather than from the header-bidding wrapper on its own.

The audit question is not whether a scanning feature exists. It is whether scanning is switched on, whether it covers the inventory the publisher actually runs, and whether anyone has confirmed it is working rather than assuming it is. A control that is enabled on one ad unit but not another, or enabled in the platform but bypassed on the header-bidding path, leaves a real opening.

A few signs point to scanning that is off, partial, or unconfirmed:
  • No creative scanning or ad quality control is enabled in Microsoft Monetize, or its status cannot be confirmed
  • Header-bidding demand routes to the page with no pre-auction or real-time scanning module in place
  • Scanning is enabled on some ad units or some demand paths but not others, leaving inventory uncovered
  • The publisher cannot say when scanning was last verified to be catching malicious creatives
You may want to confirm that creative scanning is enabled across the inventory and demand paths the publisher runs, not only on a subset. One approach is to list each demand path, note whether scanning covers it, and check the platform's quality reporting for evidence that the scanning is catching and stopping bad creatives rather than passing everything through.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, an auto-redirect that fires mid-article sends a reader off the page they came to read, and the reader blames the site rather than the creative. You may want to confirm that scanning covers the high-traffic article and section-front placements specifically. Those placements carry the most impressions and the most exposure if a malicious creative gets through.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a malicious creative or a redirect that interrupts a shopper erodes the trust the site depends on to convert. You may want to confirm scanning covers the product and category pages, where an interruption costs both the user's trust and a sale that was closer to completion than on editorial pages.

learn_more.source_title: Confiant Ad Quality Module for Prebid Server
GeoEdge Realtime Module for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Microsoft Monetize's detailed configuration documentation for creative auditing and ad quality controls is in the Microsoft Advertising partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether creative scanning is enabled in Microsoft Monetize and on any header-bidding path, and confirm it covers the inventory and demand the publisher runs rather than a subset.

platform_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the creative auditing and ad quality settings in the Microsoft Monetize console and record whether scanning for malware, auto-redirects, and policy violations is enabled.
Note which inventory and demand paths the scanning is configured to cover, and whether any ad units or sources are left out.
Open the platform's creative quality or audit reporting and record whether it shows creatives being flagged and stopped, which is evidence the scanning is active rather than dormant.
Ask the publisher when scanning was last confirmed to be catching malicious creatives, and record the answer.

header_bidding.label: Header-bidding configuration
steps: If the publisher runs Prebid or another header-bidding wrapper, list the demand paths it carries and note whether a pre-auction or real-time scanning module is in place for each.
Confirm whether the scanning module covers the same inventory the platform-level scanning covers, or whether the header-bidding path is an uncovered gap.
Record any demand path that reaches the page with no scanning between the bid and the served creative.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether creative scanning for malware, auto-redirects, and policy violations is enabled in Microsoft Monetize, whether it covers every inventory and demand path the publisher runs, and whether reporting shows it actively flagging and stopping creatives. A setup where scanning is enabled, covers all paths, and shows evidence of catching bad creatives passes. A setup where scanning is off, covers only a subset of inventory or demand, or cannot be confirmed to be working is a fail recorded with the paths left uncovered.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether creative scanning is enabled, which paths it covers, and whether reporting shows it stopping bad creatives above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs creative scanning that is off, covers only a subset of inventory, or cannot be confirmed to be working, so malicious or policy-violating creatives can reach the audience unchecked. A creative carrying malware or an auto-redirect that serves to readers harms the users {{site_name}} is responsible for and weakens its standing with the demand partners routing through the platform. {{voice_recommend}} enabling creative scanning for malware, auto-redirects, and policy violations across every inventory and demand path, including any header-bidding path through a pre-auction or real-time module, and confirming through the platform's quality reporting that the scanning is catching and stopping bad creatives. After enabling it, recheck the reporting to confirm the coverage holds across all paths.

audit_checks.title: Flagged creatives are logged but left active rather than blocked
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Scanning that flags a bad creative but does not block it gives the publisher a record of the problem without preventing it, so the malicious or policy-violating creative keeps serving. The flag is only useful when it triggers an action, so a scanning setup that detects without enforcing leaves the same exposure as no scanning at all on the creatives it catches.

educate.base:
Detecting a bad creative is only half of creative quality protection. The other half is acting on what the scan finds. A scanning setup can run in two modes. In a monitoring mode it inspects creatives and records what it flags, but it still allows the creative to serve. In a blocking mode it stops the flagged creative before it reaches the user. A publisher who assumes scanning protects the audience, while the setup is only monitoring, has a log of problems and an audience that still saw them.

Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) applies creative auditing and can disapprove or block creatives that fail its quality controls, and a publisher's own policy controls decide what gets blocked rather than merely noted. On the header-bidding path, the distinction is built into the module choice. The GeoEdge real-time module documented for Prebid blocks bad ads such as automatic redirects, malware, and offensive creatives before they serve, and the Confiant module documented for Prebid Server scans bid responses pre-auction so a flagged bid can be excluded rather than allowed to win. In each case the protection depends on the setup being configured to block, not only to inspect.

The audit question is whether the flags lead to a stopped creative. A publisher may have scanning that fires correctly and a queue of flagged creatives that never get blocked. The mode may be monitor-only, no one may review the queue, or the block action may never have been configured. The detection works and the exposure remains.

A few signs point to flags that are logged rather than acted on:
  • Scanning runs in a monitoring or report-only mode, so flagged creatives are recorded but still allowed to serve
  • A queue or report of flagged creatives exists but no one reviews it and no creative gets blocked as a result
  • The block action is not configured, so a flag produces a log entry and nothing else
  • The publisher can show flags being raised but cannot show creatives being stopped because of them
You may want to confirm that a flagged creative leads to a blocked creative rather than only a log entry. One approach is to trace a recent flag through to its outcome, checking whether the creative was stopped, and to confirm the scanning is set to block rather than monitor on the paths the publisher cares about.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a flagged auto-redirect that keeps serving while it sits in a monitoring queue sends readers off the page until someone acts. You may want to confirm the scanning is set to block on the high-traffic placements rather than monitor, so a flag stops the creative in the moment rather than after a manual review that may lag the impressions.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a flagged creative that keeps serving on product and category pages interrupts shoppers who are close to a purchase while the flag waits in a queue. You may want to confirm blocking is active on the commercial pages so a detected problem is stopped before it costs trust and a sale, rather than only recorded.

learn_more.source_title: GeoEdge Realtime Module for Header Bidding
Confiant Ad Quality Module for Prebid Server

educate.learn_more_note: Microsoft Monetize's detailed configuration documentation for creative disapproval and blocking actions is in the Microsoft Advertising partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether the scanning is set to block flagged creatives or only to monitor them, and trace a recent flag through to confirm the creative was stopped rather than logged and left active.

platform_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the creative auditing and quality controls in the Microsoft Monetize console and record whether the setup is configured to block or disapprove flagged creatives, or only to monitor and log them.
Open the flagged-creative queue or quality report and note how many flagged creatives are still active versus blocked.
Pick a recent flag and trace its outcome, recording whether the creative was stopped or left serving.
Ask the publisher who reviews the flagged queue and how often, and record the answer.

header_bidding.label: Header-bidding configuration
steps: If a Prebid scanning module is in place, confirm whether it is configured to block flagged bids or only to record them. The same module can run in either mode.
Record whether a flagged bid on the header-bidding path is excluded from the auction or allowed to win and serve.
Note any path where flags are raised but no block action follows.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the scanning is set to block flagged creatives or only to monitor them, and whether a traced flag led to a stopped creative. A setup where scanning blocks flagged creatives and a recent flag can be shown to have stopped a creative passes. A setup where scanning only monitors, where a flagged queue goes unreviewed, or where flags produce log entries without a block is a fail recorded with the paths where detection runs without enforcement.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether scanning blocks or only monitors, and whether a traced flag led to a stopped creative above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs creative scanning that flags bad creatives but leaves them active. The mode may be monitor-only or the flagged queue may go unactioned, so the detected creatives keep serving. A flag is useful only when it stops the creative, so detection without enforcement leaves the same exposure as no scanning at all on the creatives it catches. {{voice_recommend}} setting the scanning to block flagged creatives rather than only log them, including configuring any header-bidding scanning module to exclude flagged bids, and assigning a regular review of the flagged queue so nothing sits active by default. After the change, trace a recent flag through to confirm the creative was stopped rather than recorded.
